revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:柄鏡形竪穴建物]]
[[File:Katsusaka Site-D, tatemono-30.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|Sagamihara|en|Sagamihara|qid=Q209779|lt=Sagamihara City|1=Sagamihara}}{{ill|Minami-ku, Sagamihara|en|Minami-ku, Sagamihara|qid=Q1132081|lt=Minami Ward|1=Minami-ku, Sagamihara}}[[Katsusaka Archaeological Site]] (National [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Site]]) Tatemono No. 30: E-kagami-style stone-lined pit dwelling.]]
[[File:Nashinokidaira-shikiishi Archaeological site inside.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|qid=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}{{ill|Tone District, Gunma|en|Tone District, Gunma|qid=Q1077001|lt=Tone District|1=Tone District, Gunma}}{{ill|Minakami, Gunma|en|Minakami, Gunma|qid=Q1345518|lt=Minakami Town|1=Minakami, Gunma}} Nashinokidaira stone-lined dwelling site (designated as a historic site by Gunma Prefecture)<ref>{{Cite web|和書|author=Snow Country Tourism Zone Promotion Council|url=http://snow-country.jp/?a=contents&id=520|title=Nashinokidaira Stone-Lined Dwelling Site|work=Snow Country Tourism Zone|accessdate=2022-11-02}}</ref>.]]
'''E-kagami-style pit dwelling''' (柄鏡形竪穴建物, ''e-kagami-gata tateana-tatemono'') is a type of [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]] [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]] (also known as pit-house) characterized by a protruding section at the presumed building entrance. The floor plan resembles the shape of a [[:en:hand mirror|hand mirror]] with a handle, hence the name. Many examples feature flat river stones laid on the floor, leading to the alternative names '''e-kagami-style stone-lined structure''' (柄鏡形敷石建物, ''e-kagami-gata shikiishi-tatemono'') or '''e-kagami-style stone-lined dwelling''' (柄鏡形敷石住居, ''e-kagami-gata shikiishi-jukyo''){{Sfn|Cultural Properties Department of the Agency for Cultural Affairs|2013|p=143}}.
== Overview ==
The nomenclature for these [[archaeological features]] has varied. Previously, they were called '''e-kagami-style dwelling''' (柄鏡形住居, ''e-kagami-gata jukyo''), and when associated with stone lining, they were referred to as '''stone-lined dwelling''' (敷石住居, ''shikiishi jukyo'') or '''stone-lined feature''' (敷石遺構, ''shikiishi ikou''){{Sfn|Esaka|Serizawa|Sakatsume|2005|pp=183-184}}.
However, it has become apparent that these structures cannot always be definitively identified as residential facilities. In addition, it is now recognized that even typical "pit dwellings" may have served non-residential purposes such as [[:en:workshops|workshops]]. As a result, the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] recommended in the 2013 edition of ''Excavation Guide'' that these structures be referred to as '''pit buildings''' (竪穴建物, ''tateana tatemono''). Following this policy, the e-kagami-shaped structures were renamed as '''e-kagami-style pit buildings''' or '''e-kagami-style stone-lined buildings.''' Some scholars argue that they were not ordinary residential facilities since only a few were constructed within a {{ill|Settlement|en|Settlement|qid=Q1247845|lt=Settlement|1=Settlement}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties Department of the Agency for Cultural Affairs|2013|p=143}}.
These structures are distributed from the southern [[:en:Tohoku region|Tohoku region]] (e.g., {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|qid=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}) to the eastern parts of the [[:en:Kanto region|Kanto]] and [[:en:Chubu region|Chubu region]] and appeared in the late middle Jomon period, lasting until the mid-late Jomon{{Sfn|Esaka|Serizawa|Sakatsume|2005|pp=183-184}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties Department of the Agency for Cultural Affairs|2013|p=143}}.
The first discovered example was at the [[Takagasaka Archaeological Site]] in {{ill|Machida, Tokyo|en|Machida, Tokyo|qid=Q210628|lt=Machida, Tokyo|1=Machida, Tokyo}}, now designated a National [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Site]]. This site was discovered in {{ill|1924|en|1924|qid=Q2173|lt=1924|1=1924}} (Taisho 13) when bent [[:en:burdock root|burdock root]]s in a field revealed underground stone lining<ref name=machida>{{Cite web|和書|author=General Affairs Division, Lifelong Learning Department|url=https://www.city.machida.tokyo.jp/bunka/bunka_geijutsu/bunkazai/bunkazaimeguru/kogasaka.html|title=See Japan's First Discovered Stone-Lined Dwelling at the Takagasaka Archaeological Site!|work=Machida City|date=2021-11-19|accessdate=2022-11-02}}</ref>. Subsequent excavations in {{ill|1925|en|1925|qid=Q18107|lt=1925|1=1925}} (Taisho 14) by [[Goto Moriichi]] identified it as a building site{{Sfn|Cultural Properties Department of the Agency for Cultural Affairs|2013|p=143}}. Later, Goto categorized the structures into four types based on floor plans{{Sfn|Esaka|Serizawa|Sakatsume|2005|pp=183-184}}.
== Structure ==
These pit buildings feature floors dug in circular, oval, or rectangular shapes, with [[post holes]] supporting [[wooden pillars]] to hold up a roof. A distinctive feature is the oval or rectangular recess protruding outward from one corner of the pit, which is presumed to have served as an entrance. The floor plan's resemblance to a [[:en:hand mirror|hand mirror]] with a handle (柄鏡, ''e-kagami'') inspired the structure's name. In many cases, the floor—either partially or entirely—is lined with flat river stones approximately 30 cm in diameter{{Sfn|Esaka|Serizawa|Sakatsume|2005|pp=183-184}}.
At the center of the floor, there is often a stone-surrounded {{ill|Hearth|en|Hearth|qid=Q585473|lt=Hearth|1=Hearth}} (石囲炉, ''ishi-gakoiro''), sometimes containing burnt soil that indicates frequent use, though other hearths show little evidence of utilization. {{ill|Pottery|en|Pottery|qid=Q11642|lt=Pottery|1=Pottery}} is sometimes buried near the hearth or the entrance{{Sfn|Esaka|Serizawa|Sakatsume|2005|pp=183-184}}. [[Wooden posts]] supporting the roof are positioned along the walls, with [[post holes]] placed between the stones in stone-lined structures.
== Gallery ==
<gallery> File:Kogasaka Stone Age Site.jpg|E-kagami-style pit building at the [[Takagasaka Archaeological Site]]. File:Sunsawarashi Stone Age Site interior.jpg|E-kagami-style pit building at the [[Sunsawarashi Stone Age Site]]. </gallery>
